stem: A 31-year-old client at 36 weeks gestation presents to triage with sudden, severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. Based on the information in the electronic health record, complete the bowtie. Select the most likely condition, two necessary actions, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: A 31-year-old G3P2 client at 36 weeks gestation has presented to the unit with an acute obstetric emergency.
Background: Her history is significant for chronic hypertension and daily tobacco use.
Assessment: At 14:30, the client reported a sudden onset of 10/10 tearing abdominal pain with dark red vaginal bleeding. The uterus is rigid and tender. Fetal heart rate is 105 bpm with late decelerations. Maternal vitals are: BP 158/104 mmHg, HR 122/min, RR 26/min.
Recommendation: My primary suspicion is placental abruption. We must prepare for an emergent Cesarean section, establish large-bore IV access, and notify the neonatal team.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content: No lab data available at this time.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: No radiology data available at this time.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: No medications administered at this time.

actionOptions.text: Prepare for an emergent Cesarean section
Place client in a high-Fowler's position

hoverRationales.c1: Clinical Flaw: This condition typically presents with painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. The client's agonizing, tearing pain and dark red bleeding are inconsistent with this diagnosis.
hoverRationales.c2: Pathophysiology: Premature separation of the placenta from the uterine wall causes bleeding, uterine irritability (pain, board-like tone), and compromises fetal oxygenation, leading to distress. The client's risk factors and symptoms align with this pathophysiology.
hoverRationales.c3: Clinical Flaw: While uterine rupture involves severe pain, it often presents with a loss of uterine tone and palpable fetal parts, not a board-like, tense uterus. It is also less common without a prior uterine scar.
hoverRationales.a1: Pathophysiology: Placental abruption severely compromises uteroplacental blood flow, leading to fetal hypoxia. Emergent delivery via Cesarean section is required to save the fetus and control maternal hemorrhage.
hoverRationales.a2: Pathophysiology: Significant maternal blood loss, both visible and concealed, is a major risk with placental abruption. Large-bore IV access is critical for rapid fluid and blood product administration to prevent hypovolemic shock.
hoverRationales.a3: Clinical Flaw: This position is not a priority and may decrease venous return, potentially worsening hypotension in a client at risk for hemorrhage. A side-lying position is preferred to maximize uterine perfusion.
hoverRationales.a4: Clinical Flaw: Performing a digital vaginal exam is contraindicated with undiagnosed bleeding until placenta previa is ruled out by ultrasound. This action could cause catastrophic hemorrhage if a previa is present.
hoverRationales.p1: Pathophysiology: Placental separation directly impacts fetal oxygen supply, making continuous monitoring essential to detect and respond to signs of worsening fetal distress, such as bradycardia and late decelerations.
hoverRationales.p2: Pathophysiology: Placental abruption can trigger disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) due to the release of large amounts of thromboplastin from the placental site into maternal circulation. Monitoring for signs like oozing from IV sites is crucial.
hoverRationales.p3: Clinical Flaw: Measuring serum uric acid is relevant for assessing preeclampsia severity, not for the acute management of a hemorrhagic emergency like placental abruption. It is not a priority parameter.
hoverRationales.p4: Clinical Flaw: Assessing deep tendon reflexes and clonus is a key component of a neurological assessment for preeclampsia or magnesium toxicity. It is not a priority parameter for placental abruption.
